===== docs/data/observability-contract.md =====
1	# Observability Contract
3	Parent index: [Data](./!INDEX.md)
5	## Purpose and ownership
7	Observability is an active `tools/data_sync` source-of-truth domain. The editable contract is the TOML source set below. `data-sync` validates the cross-file model and generates all language/runtime metadata and the generated contract reference. Runtime emitters consume generated metadata; generated outputs are never edited manually.
9	## Source files
11	```text
12	shared/contracts/observability/schema.toml
13	shared/contracts/observability/services.toml
14	shared/contracts/observability/events.toml
15	shared/contracts/observability/fields.toml
16	shared/contracts/observability/redaction.toml
17	shared/contracts/observability/retention_tiers.toml
18	shared/contracts/observability/diagnostic_bundle.toml
19	```
21	Together these files define the envelope, service registry, event eligibility and defaults, context/free-form fields, redaction policy, retention tiers, and diagnostic-bundle contract. No service may add a local event definition or policy that changes these generated rules.
23	## Generated outputs
25	```text
26	shared/go/observabilityevent/contract_generated.go
27	client/scripts/generated/observability/contract_generated.gd
28	services/api-server/app/lib/observability/contract_generated.rb
29	shared/contracts/observability/generated/contract.json
30	docs/observability/generated/contract-reference.md
31	```
33	The generated Markdown reference is a read-only rendered reference. It changes only through the observability data-sync generator.
35	## Commands
37	From the repository root:
39	```bash
40	data-sync -validate -observability
41	data-sync -diff -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
42	data-sync -push -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
43	data-sync -check -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
44	```
46	`-validate` reads and cross-validates the source contract without writing. `-diff` renders every selected output and reports drift. `-push` writes selected generated outputs. `-check` renders without writing and fails when any selected output differs.
48	## Validation rules
50	Validation covers:
52	- one valid schema/envelope definition and supported schema version;
53	- unique service keys and emitted names;
54	- unique event names, categories, levels, service allowlists, trace requirements, and bridge-only declarations;
55	- context field names, types, UUID requirements, and limits;
56	- free-form key/value/count/size limits;
57	- redaction exact/fragment rules and rejection of ambiguous actions;
58	- retention tier references and file policy values;
59	- diagnostic-bundle field/event references;
60	- deterministic output ordering and complete configured target files.
62	Generation fails rather than silently dropping an unsupported language or output. The Ruby selector is observability-specific; JSON and docs selectors apply to observability as well as realtime wire.
64	## Generation ownership and consumers
66	`tools/data_sync/config.toml` owns the `[sot.observability]` source list and the `observability.go`, `observability.gds`, `observability.ruby`, `observability.json`, and `observability.docs` targets. The tool implementation and generators are the only owners of generated output content.
68	Consumers are:
70	- Go services through `shared/go/observabilityevent` and service `logging.Emit` boundaries;
71	- the Godot client through `observability_emitter.gd` and generated GDScript constants;
72	- API Rails through `Observability::Emitter`, `WorkerRuntime`, and `PumaHooks`;
73	- diagnostic-aggregator through generated Go observability metadata;
74	- handwritten service/data docs through this document and the generated contract reference.
76	## Failure modes
78	- malformed TOML or a missing source file fails validation;
79	- duplicate service/event/field/retention identifiers fail validation;
80	- an event referencing an unknown service, field, or retention tier fails validation;
81	- invalid trace/UUID, field type, redaction, or limit declarations fail validation;
82	- unsupported selector combinations fail CLI parsing;
83	- generated output drift causes `-check` to exit nonzero;
84	- a runtime emitter rejects an event, unsafe value, or oversized record according to generated rejection codes;
85	- a writer failure degrades the service-owned runtime but must not cause the workflow to raise or serialize the rejected/private payload.
87	The contract pipeline and runtime writer failures are separate: data-sync failure blocks a generated-contract change; a runtime write failure is a bounded service degradation.
89	## Rule against manual edits
91	Do not edit any generated output directly. Change the owning TOML source, run validation, review the diff, push the selected outputs, and run the check command. A manual edit to a generated Go, GDScript, Ruby, JSON, or Markdown file is drift and must be overwritten by the next push.

100	tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_toml.py
101	tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_validate.py
102	tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_sync.py

105	tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_gds.py
106	tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_ruby.py
107	tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_json.py
108	tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_docs.py
109	tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_toml.py
110	tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_validate.py

114	```

===== docs/devtools/client/devtools-window.md =====
1	# Devtools Window
3	Parent index: [Client](./!INDEX.md)
5	## Purpose
7	This document describes the client devtools window: the Godot debug window used to inspect live gameplay state, send server-authoritative debug commands, select debug targets, request placement-based debug spawns, and toggle client-only diagnostic overlays.
9	## Overview
11	The devtools window is a client-side development tool, not player-facing HUD. It is opened through `DevToggle0`, instantiated lazily by `DevtoolsWindowController`, and displayed as an always-on-top Godot `Window` scene.
13	The window owns presentation and control collection only. It does not apply gameplay mutations locally. Button presses, selectors, telemetry source changes, and checkbox changes emit signals. Those signals are routed through the client devtools context stack into either:
15	* generated non-command packet dictionaries sent over their owning client networking path
16	* runtime debug command payloads built by `GameplayDebugFlow` or `DevConnectionService` and sent through `ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet()` over `sr.tooling`
17	* placement flows that collect a server-space position or direction before sending a debug spawn packet
18	* client-only overlay toggles such as server hitbox drawing
19	* normal target-selection packets when setting or clearing the canonical game target
21	Server-authoritative debug effects remain owned by the game server. The client window may request invincibility, infinite lives, world-freeze changes, player-freeze changes, player kill/respawn, score/lives edits, entity spawns, pickup spawns, bullet streams, and entity clears, but the server decides whether the command is valid and how it mutates gameplay state.
23	## Debug-only scope
25	The devtools window is debug tooling for active gameplay sessions. It is separate from production HUD, menu flow, player progression, normal gameplay input, and client presentation state.
27	The window may display raw lane-applied gameplay dictionaries, debug status, target rows, player lifecycle rows, pickup selector values, and overlay toggles. It should not translate these into player-facing UI, user progression features, or permanent gameplay state.
29	Client debug input is gated by `client/scripts/devtools/dev_tools_build_flags.gd`. When `public_build` is enabled, the configured `DevToggle0` through `DevToggle9` input events are erased from the `InputMap`. The server-side command path has its own `nodevtools` build gate; disabling server devtools prevents command handling even if a client can still construct debug packets.
31	Runtime command contexts also require gameplay state before sending most debug actions. If the client has not received gameplay state, or the relevant connection service is not configured, command requests are ignored.
33	## Server authority
35	The window follows a request-only model.
37	Server-owned gameplay mutations include:
39	* invincibility
40	* infinite lives
41	* world freeze
42	* granular world freeze targets
43	* player freeze
44	* kill player
45	* respawn player
46	* set score
47	* add score
48	* set lives
49	* add lives
50	* spawn asteroid
51	* spawn pickup
52	* spawn player
53	* spawn bullet
54	* begin continuous bullet stream
55	* clear bullets
56	* clear asteroids
58	The window delegates runtime debug command payload construction to `GameplayDebugFlow` or `DevConnectionService`, which include `request_id` and `trace_id` before sending through `ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet()` over `sr.tooling`. It does not directly edit `WorldSync`, player nodes, asteroid nodes, bullet nodes, score values, lives values, or lane-applied server-state read models.
60	Tooling command results and errors return through `ToolingPacketRouter`. Developer readouts continue to use their existing routes.
62	The server remains authoritative for pickup spawn validity. The pickup selector is populated from the client pickup presentation catalog so available debug choices match presentation data, but the selector does not make the client authoritative over pickup types.
64	The server hitbox checkbox is the main exception because it toggles a client-only diagnostic overlay. It does not send a server command and does not mutate gameplay. It only controls whether already-synced server collision telemetry is drawn by the devtools overlay.
66	## Client presentation
68	The window scene lives at:
70	```text
71	client/scenes/devtools/devtools_window.tscn
72	```
74	The root script lives at:
76	```text
77	client/scripts/devtools/devtools_window.gd
78	```
80	`devtools_window.gd` remains the window composition and signal surface. Its public refresh methods are preserved as thin delegation methods for existing callers. `DevtoolsWindowTargetSelectors` owns repeated player-target selector population, selection preservation, game-target fallback, and non-counter selector updates. `DevtoolsWindowCounterControls` owns score/lives input validation, signal emission, and counter target refreshes. `DevtoolsWindowSpawnControls` owns pickup initialization and spawn/respawn control wiring. `DevtoolsWindowTelemetry` owns telemetry source selector setup, selected-source state, raw local/target rendering, sorted key output, JSON rendering, and float formatting. These seams operate on the window's existing controls and signals; they do not own gameplay mutation.
82	The window is titled `Space Rocks Devtools`, opens centered, stays on top, and hides instead of freeing itself when closed.
84	The current window surface includes:
86	* server collision telemetry checkbox
87	* pickup spawn selector and spawn-pickup button
88	* invincible toggle and target/status selector
89	* infinite-lives toggle and target/status selector
90	* world-freeze toggle and status label
91	* granular freeze buttons for asteroids, bullets, spawns, and collisions
92	* player-freeze toggle and target/status selector
93	* kill-player target selector
94	* spawn-player selector
95	* respawn-player selector
96	* spawn-asteroid and spawn-bullet placement buttons
97	* clear-asteroids and clear-bullets buttons
98	* set/add score controls
99	* set/add lives controls
100	* game-target selector with set and clear buttons
101	* local-player telemetry panel
102	* target telemetry panel
104	Status labels use `Active` and `Inactive` wording for debug statuses. Player lifecycle selectors may still use lifecycle wording such as `ALIVE` and `DEAD`.
106	## Commands and controls
108	`DevToggle0` toggles the devtools window.
110	Window controls use signals from `devtools_window.gd`. `DevtoolsWindowController` preserves the latest known state so the window can be created after gameplay state or debug status has already arrived.
112	Player-targeted controls resolve through `DevtoolsTargetResolver` and can use:
114	* an explicit selected player
115	* `Game Target`, when the canonical gameplay target is a player
116	* `All Players`, where the command supports `target_scope=all_players`
117	* the local player fallback where the command path supports fallback
119	`All Players` is represented as a scope, not as a fake player ID. It emits `target_scope=all_players` with an empty `target_player_id`.
121	Player-only commands do not resolve non-player canonical targets. If `Game Target` points at an asteroid, bullet, pickup, or enemy, player-only commands such as kill, invincible, infinite lives, player freeze, score edits, and lives edits do not emit an effective player command.
123	World-freeze controls use `toggle_debug_freeze_world`. The main world-freeze button sends the default/global freeze request. Granular buttons pass a `freeze_target` value:
125	```text
126	asteroids
127	bullets
128	spawns
129	collisions
130	```
132	Score and lives inputs must parse as integers before a command is emitted. Empty or invalid integer fields are ignored by the window.
134	Spawn and respawn buttons do not immediately mutate gameplay. They route into placement or respawn request flows:
136	* asteroid, bullet, pickup, and player spawn controls request placement
137	* pickup placement includes the selected `pickup_type`
138	* player spawn placement may include a selected target player slot
139	* continuous bullet stream placement is owned by the devtools session placement flow, not the window itself
140	* respawn requests use the selected target scope and player ID
142	Set/Clear Game Target uses the normal target packet path to update the canonical player target. This is a target-selection request, not a debug mutation by itself.
144	## Target rows and read models
146	`DevtoolsPlayerTargetModel` builds window target rows from applied gameplay lane state and debug status.
148	Inputs include:
150	* `self_id`
151	* `server_players`
152	* `player_sessions`
153	* `server_asteroids`
154	* `server_bullets`
155	* `server_pickups`
156	* `server_enemies` or `enemies`
157	* `player_lifecycle`
158	* `debug_statuses`
159	* canonical `target_kind`
160	* canonical `target_id`
162	The model creates separate row sets for different control types:
164	* kill-player rows
165	* respawn-player rows
166	* invincibility rows
167	* infinite-lives rows
168	* player-frozen rows
169	* active-player rows for score/lives controls
170	* game-target options
172	Feature rows display per-player debug status as `Active` or `Inactive`. Lifecycle rows use lifecycle state where available, and otherwise fall back to whether a player exists in `server_players`.
174	The canonical target supports display and telemetry for:
176	```text
177	player
178	enemy
179	pickup
180	asteroid
181	bullet
182	```
184	Only `player` canonical targets resolve into player-command targets.
186	## Telemetry behavior
188	The devtools window owns raw state inspection. It currently exposes two raw telemetry panels:
190	* local player telemetry
191	* current target telemetry
193	Each panel can select a source:
195	```text
196	lane-applied state readback
197	session lane readback
198	```
200	The `lane-applied state readback` source reads applied gameplay entity dictionaries from the accumulated devtools gameplay readmodel. For the local player, this reads from `server_players`. For targets, it can read from player, asteroid, bullet, pickup, or enemy dictionaries according to canonical `target_kind`; those dictionaries may originate from world, lifecycle, hot, session, or future entity-family lanes.
202	The `session lane readback` source reads durable player/session dictionaries from `player_sessions`. It is only meaningful for player targets. Non-player targets render unavailable output for this source.
204	Telemetry output is intentionally generic. Dictionary keys are sorted and rendered as key/value lines. Arrays and dictionaries are rendered as JSON strings. Floats are rendered to four decimal places. Target telemetry includes `target_kind` and `target_id` above the raw body when a target is selected.
206	Debug status packets update:
208	* global/world freeze status
209	* granular freeze status
210	* per-player invincible status
211	* per-player infinite-lives status
212	* per-player frozen status
214	The window telemetry panels are separate from the world telemetry overlay. The world telemetry overlay is a glanceable metrics surface; the devtools window is a raw inspection and command surface.
216	## Runtime flow
218	The current client flow is:
220	```text
221	DevToggle0
222	-> DevtoolsHotkeyContext
223	-> GameplayDevtoolsContext.toggle_devtools_window()
224	-> DevtoolsWindowController.toggle_window()
225	-> DevtoolsWindowScene.instantiate()
226	-> devtools_window.gd
227	```
229	Window command flow is:
231	```text
232	devtools_window.gd signal
233	-> DevtoolsWindowController
234	-> DevtoolsWindowActionContext
235	-> DevtoolsCommandContext / DevtoolsPlacementContext / DevtoolsOverlayContext
236	-> GameplayDebugFlow / DevConnectionService / overlay implementation
237	-> command payload with request_id and trace_id -> ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet() over sr.tooling
238	-> generated packet or client-only overlay update
239	```
241	Lane-native gameplay output and debug status flow back into the window through:
243	```text
244	lane/debug packet
245	-> RealtimeRouter
246	-> DevtoolsLaneStateAdapter.build_state(...)
247	-> GameplayComposition.apply_devtools_gameplay_state(devtools_state)
248	-> GameplayDevtoolsContext.apply_gameplay_state(devtools_state)
249	-> DevtoolsGameplayStateContext.apply_gameplay_state(devtools_state)
250	-> DevtoolsDisplayRefreshFlow
251	-> DevtoolsWindowController
252	-> devtools_window.gd
253	```
255	Placement requests flow through the gameplay shell because placement needs the current world coordinate conversion:
257	```text
258	window spawn control
259	-> DevtoolsWindowController
260	-> DevtoolsPlacementContext
261	-> GameplayShellFlow placement route
262	-> DevToolsSessionFlow
263	-> DebugClickPlacementFlow or DebugContinuousBulletSpawnFlow
264	-> placement result
265	-> GameplayShellFlow.handle_devtools_placement_result()
266	-> GameplayDevtoolsContext.handle_placement_result()
267	-> DevConnectionService
268	-> debug spawn packet
269	-> ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet() over sr.tooling
270	```
272	## Build and runtime gates
274	Client-side gate:
276	```text
277	client/scripts/devtools/dev_tools_build_flags.gd
278	```
280	This file removes `DevToggle0` through `DevToggle9` input events when `public_build` is true.
282	Server-side gate:
284	```text
285	nodevtools
286	```
288	Server `nodevtools` builds disable server command handling. The client window must not be treated as authority even when it can still construct a debug packet.
290	Runtime gates:
292	* command contexts require gameplay state for most actions
293	* connection-backed commands require a configured connection service
294	* respawn and spawn helpers refuse empty packet builds
295	* player-only commands refuse empty effective player targets
296	* non-player canonical targets do not resolve into player-only command targets
298	## Code map

302	```text

376	Important non-ownership boundaries:
378	* `devtools_window.gd` owns window composition, public refresh routing, UI action handlers, and signal emission, not gameplay mutation.
379	* `DevtoolsWindowTargetSelectors` owns repeated player-target selector population, selection preservation, game-target fallback, and non-counter selector updates.
380	* `DevtoolsWindowCounterControls` owns score/lives input validation, counter signal emission, and the four counter target selectors.
381	* `DevtoolsWindowSpawnControls` owns pickup initialization and spawn/respawn control wiring while preserving the window's placement-request signals.
382	* `DevtoolsWindowTelemetry` owns telemetry selector setup/state and raw telemetry rendering/formatting.
383	* `DevtoolsWindowController` owns window lifecycle, latest cached UI state, and target-scope conversion.
384	* `DevtoolsCommandContext` owns command routing into debug packet send paths.
385	* `DevtoolsPlacementContext` owns placement request handoff, not mouse/world coordinate conversion.
386	* `DevToolsSessionFlow` owns debug placement interaction and continuous bullet placement routing.
387	* `WorldSync` and gameplay runtime code expose read-only state needed by devtools but do not own devtools rendering.
388	* server hitbox rendering is client devtools presentation; authoritative shape/state data comes from synced server/debug data.
390	## Tests and verification
392	Relevant client tests include:
394	```text
395	client/tests/unit/devtools/devtools_window_test.gd
396	client/tests/unit/devtools/test_devtools_window_telemetry.gd
397	client/tests/unit/devtools/test_devtools_window_target_selectors.gd
398	client/tests/unit/test_devtools_window_controller.gd
399	client/tests/unit/test_devtools_player_target_model.gd
400	```
402	Current test coverage verifies:
404	* granular freeze buttons emit the expected freeze targets
405	* debug status updates granular freeze labels
406	* pickup selector values are populated from the pickup catalog and default to `1_up`
407	* telemetry selectors initialize with stable defaults and preserve source selection
408	* local and target telemetry render empty states, sorted keys, JSON values, and four-decimal floats
409	* player-target selectors preserve selected metadata, fall back to the game target where applicable, clear empty sets, retain local-player labels/metadata, and update all shared score/lives selectors consistently
410	* explicit selected players override canonical game targets
411	* player canonical targets resolve for player-only commands
412	* non-player canonical targets do not emit player-only commands
413	* `All Players` emits `target_scope=all_players` with an empty player ID
414	* target-scope routing is preserved for respawn, toggle, score, and lives actions
415	* target row and telemetry model behavior is derived from devtools gameplay readmodels and debug status

426	## Notes
428	The devtools window should remain a narrow control and inspection surface. New controls should route through the existing devtools contexts, generated packet helpers, placement route, or overlay route instead of adding local gameplay mutation.
430	Raw telemetry should stay generic. Field-specific gameplay interpretation belongs in the owning gameplay, protocol, data, service, or systems-design documentation rather than in custom devtools-window formatting.

===== docs/devtools/server/spawn-tools.md =====
1	# Spawn Tools
3	Parent index: [Server](./!INDEX.md)
5	## Purpose
7	This document describes the server-side devtools spawn tooling.
9	It covers the debug command surface that spawns players, asteroids, bullets, and pickups into an active game instance. It documents server authority, command behavior, telemetry, build/runtime gates, implementation paths, and verification coverage.
11	## Overview
13	Spawn tools are development-only mutation commands routed through the game server devtools surface.
15	`GameplayDebugFlow` and `DevConnectionService` build spawn command payloads with `request_id` and `trace_id`; `ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet()` sends them through `sr.tooling`; and server networking/tooling applies policy, room, and capability preflight before decoding them as `devtools.DebugCommand` and dispatching to the existing devtools controller. The client may request a position, direction, entity type, pickup type, or target player id, but the server owns the actual mutation and the resulting authoritative state.
17	The current spawn packet surface is:
19	```text
20	debug_spawn_entity
21	debug_spawn_pickup
22	```
24	`debug_spawn_entity` handles these entity types:
26	```text
27	player
28	asteroid
29	bullet
30	```
32	`debug_spawn_pickup` handles pickup entities separately because pickup spawning needs `pickup_type` instead of the generic `entity_type` field.
34	Spawn tools are not a production gameplay spawning API. They are debug commands that intentionally converge on real server mutation paths where practical.
36	## Debug-only scope
38	Spawn tools are devtools-only behavior.
40	They are used for:
42	* creating a debug player ship at a requested position
43	* creating an asteroid at a requested position
44	* creating a bullet at a requested position
45	* creating a pickup at a requested position
46	* testing world sync, state projection, collision bodies, pickup presentation, target readouts, and placement flows
47	* reproducing entity-specific gameplay or rendering problems without waiting for normal gameplay conditions
49	They are not used for:
51	* normal timed asteroid spawning
52	* asteroid fragment spawning after destruction
53	* drop-table pickup spawning
54	* normal weapon fire
55	* player join flow
56	* normal respawn flow
57	* room membership authority
58	* match lifecycle authority
59	* durable player-data mutation
61	Respawn, clear-entity, and continuous bullet stream tooling are adjacent devtools features with separate command behavior. Spawn tools may share packet fields and placement flows with them, but this document covers only one-shot spawn commands.
63	## Server authority
65	The game server remains authoritative for spawned runtime state.
67	The client may send:
69	```text
70	entity_type
71	pickup_type
72	x
73	y
74	has_direction
75	direction_x
76	direction_y
77	target_player_id
78	```
80	The server decides:
82	* whether the packet type is a spawn command
83	* whether the command can be decoded
84	* whether the session has an active room and required tooling capability
85	* the current `GamePlayerID` value, when present, for command-specific owner or fallback behavior
86	* which spawn helper handles the requested entity type
87	* whether a requested target player id is valid
88	* whether a new debug player id can be allocated
89	* how positions are normalized into toroidal world space
90	* how fallback directions are selected
91	* how runtime ids are allocated
92	* how the new entity enters the authoritative entity store
93	* what family-specific realtime lanes later project to clients
95	The spawn command path does not give the client direct write access to server entity maps. Client placement requests route through server command handlers and game-owned mutation helpers.
97	## Client presentation
99	The client owns spawn UI and placement input, not spawn authority.
101	Client-side devtools can start placement actions from hotkeys or the devtools window. The client converts mouse placement into server coordinates, adds `request_id` and `trace_id`, and sends the appropriate command through `ClientConnectionService.send_tooling_packet()` over `sr.tooling`.
103	Current client placement actions include:
105	```text
106	spawn_player
107	spawn_asteroid
108	spawn_bullet
109	spawn_pickup
110	```
112	The server does not send a special spawn-confirmation packet. Confirmation is observed through normal authoritative readback:
114	```text
115	world lane ship records
116	asteroids_lifecycle creates
117	bullets_lifecycle creates
118	world lane pickup records
119	session lane player records
120	debug_status packets
121	event_batch where applicable
122	```
124	`debug_spawn_entity` does not create gameplay truth locally. The server mutates authoritative state, and spawned bullets appear later as `bullets_lifecycle` creates while spawned asteroids appear later as `asteroids_lifecycle` creates.
126	## Command routing
128	Spawn commands use the tooling route before normal gameplay packet handling.
130	The current high-level route is:
132	```text
133	client command payload with request_id and trace_id
134	-> sr.tooling
135	-> networking/tooling policy, room, and capability preflight
136	-> packet decode into devtools.DebugCommand
137	-> existing devtools Controller.HandleCommand
138	-> capability-specific handler
139	-> game.Control
140	-> correlated tooling_command_result or tooling_error
141	-> lane-native realtime projection
142	```
144	The tooling command policy recognizes:
146	```text
147	debug_spawn_entity
148	debug_spawn_pickup
149	```
151	The route rejects commands without a current room or required capability. A missing `GamePlayerID` does not block room-global or explicit-target command dispatch; command-specific owner or fallback requirements are enforced by the handler. Decode failures return `tooling_error` and stop command execution.
153	`Controller.HandleCommand` dispatches spawn commands to:
155	```text
156	debug_spawn_entity -> handleDebugSpawnEntity
157	debug_spawn_pickup -> handleDebugSpawnPickup
158	```
160	`handleDebugSpawnEntity` then dispatches by `entity_type`:
162	```text
163	player   -> applyDebugSpawnPlayer
164	bullet   -> applyDebugSpawnBullet
165	asteroid -> applyDebugSpawnAsteroid
166	```
168	Unknown generic spawn entity types are logged as unimplemented and otherwise consumed as handled devtools commands.
170	## Packet fields
172	The shared debug command struct contains the spawn fields used by both generic entity spawn and pickup spawn:
174	```text
175	type
176	request_id
177	trace_id
178	target_player_id
179	entity_type
180	pickup_type
181	x
182	y
183	has_direction
184	direction_x
185	direction_y
186	```
188	`debug_spawn_entity` uses:
190	```text
191	type = debug_spawn_entity
192	entity_type
193	x
194	y
195	has_direction
196	direction_x
197	direction_y
198	target_player_id
199	```
201	`debug_spawn_pickup` uses:
203	```text
204	type = debug_spawn_pickup
205	pickup_type
206	x
207	y
208	```
210	`target_player_id` is meaningful for debug player spawn. It is not the generic gameplay target model.
212	`has_direction`, `direction_x`, and `direction_y` are meaningful for asteroid and bullet spawn. If direction is not supplied or is a zero vector, the server uses a random unit-vector fallback.
214	## Position and direction handling
216	Spawn positions are normalized through toroidal world space before most spawned entities are created.
218	Generic entity spawn requests use:
220	```text
221	SpawnEntityRequest.Position
222	-> space.NormalizePosition
223	```
225	Pickup spawn uses:
227	```text
228	DebugCommand x/y
229	-> space.NormalizePosition
230	```
232	Direction handling uses:
234	```text
235	SpawnEntityRequest.DirectionOr(fallback)
236	```
238	The direction resolver:
240	* uses the requested direction only when `has_direction` is true
241	* rejects a zero requested direction
242	* normalizes accepted requested direction
243	* otherwise returns a normalized fallback direction
245	Fallback directions come from the game spawner through the Control adapter.
247	## Player spawn behavior
249	Debug player spawn creates or replaces a server-side player session and active ship for a debug player id.
251	The current player spawn flow is:
253	```text
254	handleDebugSpawnEntity
255	-> applyDebugSpawnPlayer
256	-> resolveDebugSpawnPlayerID
257	-> Control player-spawn capability
258	-> ensure camera view when absent
259	-> apply supplied config only when both dimensions are positive
260	```
262	If `target_player_id` is supplied, the server normalizes it to the `player-N` format and reserves that id through the game-owned id reservation seam. Valid examples include:
264	```text
265	player-1
266	Player-1
267	```
269	The normalized stored form is:
271	```text
272	player-1
273	```
275	Invalid ids are rejected.
277	If `target_player_id` is not supplied, the server allocates the first available debug gameplay player id from the bounded player id range. The allocation checks both player sessions and active player ships before reserving a candidate.
279	Player spawn creates a player session through:
281	```text
282	Control player/session helper
283	```
285	It then creates the active ship through:
287	```text
288	Control player spawn helper
289	```
291	The devtools player ship path resets respawn cooldown, creates a new ship from the session, stores it in `game.entities.Players`, and ensures a camera view exists. Debug-spawned players use the dummy camera config from the devtools player-camera helper.
293	Debug player spawn does not create a websocket session, authenticate a user, or update durable player data. It creates runtime game state for development inspection.
295	## Asteroid spawn behavior
297	Debug asteroid spawn builds an asteroid spawn plan, then applies it through the same game-owned asteroid application seam used by normal asteroid creation.
299	The current asteroid flow is:
301	```text
302	handleDebugSpawnEntity
303	-> applyDebugSpawnAsteroid
304	-> buildDebugAsteroidSpawnPlan
305	-> Control.ApplyAsteroidSpawnPlan
306	-> game.applyAsteroidSpawn
307	```
309	The debug asteroid spawn plan uses:
311	```text
312	EntityType = asteroid
313	Reason     = debug_asteroid
314	Position   = normalized requested position
315	Velocity   = requested or fallback direction * random asteroid speed
316	Size       = random integer from 1 through 4
317	Variant    = weighted debug-spawn variant index
318	```
320	Variant selection uses the asteroid variant catalog helper:
322	```go
323	asteroids.RandomDebugSpawnVariantIndex()
324	```
326	The devtools path must not reintroduce raw random variant pools. Debug asteroid spawn should remain aligned with the shared asteroid variant contract.
328	Asteroid id allocation and runtime entity insertion are owned by root game code:
330	```text
331	game.applyAsteroidSpawn
332	```
334	The spawned asteroid is stored in:
336	```text
337	game.entities.Asteroids
338	```
340	## Bullet spawn behavior
342	Debug bullet spawn creates a basic cannon projectile owned by the requesting game player id.
344	The current bullet flow is:
346	```text
347	handleDebugSpawnEntity
348	-> applyDebugSpawnBullet
349	-> Control.SpawnBullet
350	-> game.spawnDebugBullet
351	```
353	The bullet owner is the `playerID` supplied by the active networking/game session, not a client-supplied owner field.
355	The bullet spawn path requires:
357	```text
358	target game is not nil
359	owner player id is not empty
360	direction normalizes to a nonzero vector
361	```
363	The bullet spawn implementation:
365	* normalizes the requested position
366	* normalizes the requested or fallback direction
367	* derives rotation from direction
368	* uses `constants.BasicCannonProjectileSpeed`
369	* uses `constants.BasicCannonProjectileLifetime`
370	* allocates the next bullet id through the game spawner
371	* creates `runtime.NewBullet`
372	* stores it in `game.entities.Projectiles`
374	Debug bullet spawn does not use weapon slot cooldowns, ammo state, loadout state, or normal weapon fire policy. It is a direct debug projectile creation path that still mutates authoritative server runtime state.
376	## Pickup spawn behavior
378	Debug pickup spawn creates an authoritative pickup through the game pickup spawn API.
380	The current pickup flow is:
382	```text
383	handleDebugSpawnPickup
384	-> Game.SpawnPickup
385	-> Game.spawnPickupLocked
386	-> pickups.DefinitionFor
387	-> game.entities.Pickups
388	```
390	Pickup spawn uses:
392	```text
393	pickup_type
394	x
395	y
396	```
398	The server converts `pickup_type` into a `pickups.PickupType` and validates it against server pickup definitions. Unknown pickup types are rejected by the pickup spawn API and logged as ignored debug spawns.
400	A successful pickup spawn:
402	* allocates the next pickup id
403	* creates the pickup entity from the definition
404	* initializes health, age, and lifespan
405	* stores the pickup in `game.entities.Pickups`
406	* makes it available for family-specific realtime readback and pickup lifecycle handling
408	Debug pickup spawn does not record a `pickup_dropped` gameplay event. Drop-table integration owns that event for normal asteroid-drop spawns.
410	## Telemetry
412	Spawn tools use structured game logs.
414	Generic entity spawn logs include:
416	```text
417	debug player spawn ignored
418	debug player spawned
419	debug bullet spawn ignored
420	debug bullet spawned
421	debug asteroid spawn ignored
422	debug asteroid spawned
423	debug spawn entity not implemented for entity type
424	```
426	Pickup spawn logs include:
428	```text
429	debug pickup spawn ignored
430	debug pickup spawned
431	```
433	Logged fields may include:
435	```text
436	player_id
437	target_player_id
438	spawned_player_id
439	owner_player_id
440	bullet_id
441	asteroid_id
442	pickup_id
443	pickup_type
444	entity_type
445	x
446	y
447	has_direction
448	has_target_player_id
449	```
451	Tooling decode failures are reported as correlated `tooling_error` responses. There is no separate spawn telemetry packet; spawn outcomes are confirmed by normal logs and authoritative state readback.
453	## Build/runtime gates
455	Server devtools are build-gated by the devtools package.
457	Default builds use:
459	```text
460	services/game-server/internal/devtools/enabled_default.go
461	```
463	`nodevtools` builds use:
465	```text
466	services/game-server/internal/devtools/enabled_nodevtools.go
467	```
469	The package-level command gate is:
471	```text
472	devtools.ShouldHandleCommand(packetType)
473	```
475	It combines command type recognition with the current devtools build flag.
477	Spawn command files do not own this gate directly. They are implementation handlers behind devtools packet routing and command dispatch.
479	Runtime gates also apply:
481	* tooling policy requires a recognized spawn packet type
482	* the session must have the `tooling.control` capability
483	* command decode must succeed
484	* the session must have a current room
485	* `GamePlayerID` is passed through when present but is not required merely to dispatch a room-global or explicit-target command
486	* generic entity spawn must name a supported entity type
487	* player spawn must resolve or allocate a valid player id
488	* bullet spawn must have a nonempty owner id and nonzero direction
489	* pickup spawn must use a known pickup type
490	* asteroid spawn requires a live game target and successful game-owned spawn application
492	## Relationship to real gameplay implementation areas
494	Spawn tools must not become parallel gameplay implementations.
496	The intended relationship is:
498	```text
499	devtools command
500	-> debug adapter
501	-> game-owned mutation seam
502	-> normal runtime entity storage
503	-> lane-native realtime projection
504	```
506	Current examples:
508	* Debug asteroid spawn builds a debug asteroid spawn plan, then applies it through `game.applyAsteroidSpawn`.
509	* Debug pickup spawn calls `Game.SpawnPickup`, which delegates to the normal pickup creation path.
510	* Debug player spawn uses game-owned session, ship, and camera-view helpers.
511	* Debug bullet spawn uses a game-owned debug bullet helper and stores the bullet in the authoritative projectile map.
513	The devtools package may shape debug requests, validate debug-only fields, and log debug actions. It should not own scoring, normal spawn scheduling, pickup drop policy, room membership, durable profile state, or production gameplay lifecycle rules.

665	## Notes
667	`debug_spawn_entity` and `debug_spawn_pickup` are placement devtools packets on the server inbound path.
669	Debug bullet spawn intentionally bypasses normal weapon fire policy. That makes it useful for projectile diagnostics, but it should not be treated as proof that normal equipped weapon fire, ammo, cooldown, or loadout behavior is working.
671	Debug pickup spawn creates a real authoritative pickup entity, but it does not represent normal drop-table behavior.
673	Debug player spawn creates runtime player state for inspection. It does not represent a normal authenticated join path or durable player profile state.

===== docs/protocol/player-data-http-api.md =====
1	# Player Data HTTP API
3	Parent index: [Protocol](./!INDEX.md)
5	## Purpose
7	This document describes the current player-data HTTP protocol surfaces.
9	It covers the client-facing player-data HTTP facade hosted by the game-server process, the request and response shapes owned by OpenAPI, the player-data handlers that implement profile and local-profile behavior, and the internal Rails HTTP calls used for authenticated-account persistence.
11	## Overview
13	The player-data HTTP API is a JSON HTTP boundary for profile readout and local profile management.
15	This document is the detailed protocol doc for the player-data HTTP subset. The centralized API product surface map lives in [API Product Surface](api-product-surface.md).
17	Current client-facing player-data routes are hosted by the game-server process on the data-handler base URL:
19	```text
20	http://localhost:8080
21	```
23	The game server hosts these routes, but it does not own the player-data behavior behind them. It builds the player-data runtime, adapts the auth verifier, mounts the routes, and delegates request handling to `services/player-data/httpapi`.
25	Current hosted player-data routes:
27	```text
28	POST   /api/player-data/profile
29	GET    /api/player-data/local-profiles
30	POST   /api/player-data/local-profiles
31	PUT    /api/player-data/local-profiles/{local_profile_id}
32	DELETE /api/player-data/local-profiles/{local_profile_id}
33	GET    /api/player-data/local-profiles/default
34	PUT    /api/player-data/local-profiles/default
35	```
37	The hosted API is consumed by the Godot client for pregame profile readout and local pilot management. The API carries identity context, local profile identifiers, display names, default-profile selection, and normalized player stats. It does not carry live gameplay state and does not allow the client to choose guest memory, embedded SQLite, Rails, or Postgres directly.
39	HTTP request and response shapes are sourced from:
41	```text
42	shared/contracts/http/openapi.yaml
43	```
45	OpenAPI owns HTTP shape. Player-data runtime and stores own behavior behind the shape.
47	Authenticated-account stats and match-result persistence use separate internal Rails HTTP calls made by the player-data Rails adapter:
49	```text
50	POST /api/internal/player-data/stats
51	POST /internal/player-data/match-results
52	```
54	Those routes are owned by the API server. They are part of the player-data HTTP communication flow, but they are not hosted by the game-server player-data facade.
56	## Participating systems
58	```text
59	client/
60	```
62	Consumes the hosted player-data HTTP facade through `PlayerDataProfileApiClient`, `LocalPilotApiClient`, and the shared `ApiHttpClient`.
64	```text
65	services/game-server/
66	```
68	Hosts the HTTP mux on `:8080`, mounts player-data routes, builds the player-data runtime, and adapts the game-server auth verifier for authenticated profile reads.
70	```text
71	services/player-data/
72	```
74	Implements the hosted profile and local profile handlers, owns player-data runtime behavior, routes identities to stores, and calls Rails internal endpoints for authenticated-account persistence when configured.
76	```text
77	services/api-server/
78	```
80	Owns Rails auth, token verification, authenticated-account stats persistence, authenticated-account match-result persistence, and Rails/Postgres physical storage.
82	```text
83	shared/contracts/http/openapi.yaml
84	```
86	Owns the HTTP request and response contract.
88	## Authority
90	OpenAPI owns HTTP request and response shapes.
92	The game server owns route hosting for the current in-process player-data facade.
94	The player-data service owns hosted profile and local profile HTTP behavior.
96	The player-data runtime owns identity-based route selection and store delegation.
98	The API server owns authenticated-account internal HTTP endpoints and Rails/Postgres persistence.
100	The client owns request consumption and presentation flow, not the authoritative meaning of returned data.
102	The player-data HTTP API does not own:
104	```text
105	realtime packet shapes
106	runtime player-data packet schemas
107	Rails database schema
108	embedded SQLite schema
109	client UI layout
110	room admission
111	live gameplay simulation
112	match outcome calculation
113	OAuth provider behavior
114	OpenAPI runtime middleware
115	generated HTTP clients
116	```
118	## Message or request flow
120	### Hosted profile read
122	```text
123	client
124	-> POST /api/player-data/profile on game-server data-handler
125	-> services/player-data/httpapi.ProfileHandler
126	-> optional bearer-token verification for authenticated_account
127	-> playerdata.Runtime.LoadStats
128	-> selected store route
129	-> normalized profile response
130	-> client profile readout
131	```
133	The profile endpoint exists so the client can request one normalized profile payload regardless of whether the active identity is Guest, Local Profile, or Authenticated Account.
135	Request body:
137	```json
138	{
139	  "play_mode": "single_player",
140	  "identity_kind": "guest",
141	  "local_profile_id": ""
142	}
143	```
145	Supported `play_mode` values:
147	```text
148	single_player
149	multiplayer
150	multiplayer_simulation
151	```
153	Supported `identity_kind` values:
155	```text
156	guest
157	local_profile
158	authenticated_account
159	```
161	Guest profile reads require no bearer token.
163	Local Profile profile reads require a non-empty `local_profile_id` and require no bearer token.
165	Authenticated Account profile reads require:
167	```text
168	Authorization: Bearer <user token>
169	```
171	The hosted profile handler verifies the user token through the injected auth verifier. Token verification returns the authenticated `account_id` and optional display name used for profile presentation and store routing.
173	Successful response:
175	```json
176	{
177	  "profile": {
178	    "callsign": "Guest",
179	    "activity_status": "OFFLINE",
180	    "identity_kind": "guest",
181	    "stats": {
182	      "total_score": 0,
183	      "high_score": 0,
184	      "ship_deaths": 0,
185	      "games_played": 0,
186	      "wins": 0
187	    }
188	  }
189	}
190	```
192	Current presentation values:
194	| Identity kind           | Callsign source                            | Activity status |
195	| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | --------------- |
196	| `guest`                 | `Guest`                                    | `OFFLINE`       |
197	| `local_profile`         | `Local Pilot`                              | `LOCAL`         |
198	| `authenticated_account` | verified display name, or `Pilot` fallback | `ACTIVE`        |
200	If `Runtime.LoadStats` succeeds but reports no found stats, the profile handler returns zero stats.
202	The profile HTTP handler validates supported `play_mode` and supported `identity_kind` strings. It does not apply the full player-data mode/identity admission matrix before calling `Runtime.LoadStats`. Packet-dispatched player-data reads and writes validate mode/identity pairs through the player-data dispatcher.
204	Profile error responses use this shape:
206	```json
207	{
208	  "error": "invalid_request"
209	}
210	```
212	Current profile errors:
214	| Error                 | HTTP status | Meaning                                                                                                                  |
215	| --------------------- | ----------: | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
216	| `method_not_allowed`  |       `405` | Unsupported method reached the handler.                                                                                  |
217	| `invalid_request`     |       `400` | Invalid JSON, unsupported play mode, unsupported identity kind, or missing local profile ID for a local-profile request. |
218	| `unauthorized`        |       `401` | Missing, invalid, unverifiable, or unsupported authenticated-account bearer token.                                       |
219	| `profile_unavailable` |       `500` | Missing runtime or failed stats load.                                                                                    |
221	### Local profile list
223	```text
224	GET /api/player-data/local-profiles
225	```
227	This endpoint lists local profile summaries for the local pilot selector.
229	Successful response:
231	```json
232	{
233	  "profiles": [
234	    {
235	      "local_profile_id": "local-profile-...",
236	      "display_name": "Pilot_1"
237	    }
238	  ]
239	}
240	```
242	`local_profile_id` is durable local identity.
244	`display_name` is presentation data, not identity.
246	Unavailable local profile storage returns:
248	```json
249	{
250	  "error": "local_profiles_unavailable"
251	}
252	```
254	with HTTP `503`.
256	### Local profile create
258	```text
259	POST /api/player-data/local-profiles
260	```
262	This endpoint creates a new local profile with a server-generated ID.
264	Request body:
266	```json
267	{
268	  "display_name": "Pilot_1",
269	  "seed_from_guest_stats": true
270	}
271	```
273	The handler trims `display_name` and accepts only non-empty names matching:
275	```text
276	^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$
277	```
279	The handler generates IDs in this format:
281	```text
282	local-profile-<16 random bytes as hex>
283	```
285	When `seed_from_guest_stats` is `false`, the new profile starts with zero stats.
287	When `seed_from_guest_stats` is `true`, the handler asks the runtime for current Guest stats and passes those stats into local profile creation. The handler does not read guest storage directly.
289	Successful response:
291	```json
292	{
293	  "profile": {
294	    "local_profile_id": "local-profile-...",
295	    "display_name": "Pilot_1"
296	  }
297	}
298	```
300	with HTTP `201`.
302	Invalid JSON or invalid display names return `invalid_request` with HTTP `400`.
304	Unavailable local profile storage or guest-stat seeding failure returns `local_profiles_unavailable` with HTTP `503`.
306	### Local profile display-name update
308	```text
309	PUT /api/player-data/local-profiles/{local_profile_id}
310	```
312	This endpoint updates display name only.
314	Request body:
316	```json
317	{
318	  "display_name": "Pilot_2"
319	}
320	```
322	The path `local_profile_id` must be non-empty. The new `display_name` uses the same validation rule as create.
324	Successful response:
326	```json
327	{
328	  "profile": {
329	    "local_profile_id": "local-profile-...",
330	    "display_name": "Pilot_2"
331	  }
332	}
333	```
335	The local profile identity and stats stay attached to the same `local_profile_id`.
337	Missing profiles return:
339	```json
340	{
341	  "error": "local_profile_not_found"
342	}
343	```
345	with HTTP `404`.
347	### Local profile delete
349	```text
350	DELETE /api/player-data/local-profiles/{local_profile_id}
351	```
353	This endpoint deletes a local profile through the player-data runtime and local store.
355	Successful delete returns:
357	```text
358	204 No Content
359	```
361	The embedded SQLite store deletes the profile row, local stats row, and local match-result rows. If the deleted profile was the stored default, the default resets to Guest.
363	The handler requires a non-empty path `local_profile_id`.
365	Missing profiles return `local_profile_not_found` with HTTP `404`.
367	Unavailable local profile storage returns `local_profiles_unavailable` with HTTP `503`.
369	### Default local profile read
371	```text
372	GET /api/player-data/local-profiles/default
373	```
375	This endpoint reads the persisted default local identity for the local pilot flow.
377	Guest response:
379	```json
380	{
381	  "default_profile": {
382	    "identity_kind": "guest",
383	    "local_profile_id": "",
384	    "display_name": "Guest"
385	  }
386	}
387	```
389	Local profile response:
391	```json
392	{
393	  "default_profile": {
394	    "identity_kind": "local_profile",
395	    "local_profile_id": "local-profile-...",
396	    "display_name": "Pilot_1"
397	  }
398	}
399	```
401	The local store returns Guest when no default row exists, when a stored default is invalid, or when the stored local profile no longer exists.
403	Unavailable local profile storage returns `local_profiles_unavailable` with HTTP `503`.
405	### Default local profile update
407	```text
408	PUT /api/player-data/local-profiles/default
409	```
411	Guest request:
413	```json
414	{
415	  "identity_kind": "guest",
416	  "local_profile_id": ""
417	}
418	```
420	Local profile request:
422	```json
423	{
424	  "identity_kind": "local_profile",
425	  "local_profile_id": "local-profile-..."
426	}
427	```
429	The handler accepts only:
431	```text
432	identity_kind = guest
433	identity_kind = local_profile
434	```
436	Guest default requires an empty `local_profile_id`.
438	Local profile default requires a non-empty `local_profile_id`.
440	Successful response:
442	```json
443	{
444	  "default_profile": {
445	    "identity_kind": "local_profile",
446	    "local_profile_id": "local-profile-...",
447	    "display_name": "Pilot_1"
448	  }
449	}
450	```
452	Missing local profiles return `local_profile_not_found` with HTTP `404`.
454	Invalid identity/default combinations return `invalid_request` with HTTP `400`.
456	Unavailable local profile storage returns `local_profiles_unavailable` with HTTP `503`.
458	## Hosted local profile error model
460	Local profile route errors use this shape:
462	```json
463	{
464	  "error": "local_profiles_unavailable"
465	}
466	```
468	Current local profile errors:
470	| Error                        | HTTP status | Meaning                                                                                                                                           |
471	| ---------------------------- | ----------: | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
472	| `method_not_allowed`         |       `405` | Unsupported HTTP method reached the handler.                                                                                                      |
473	| `invalid_request`            |       `400` | Invalid JSON, invalid display name, invalid path value, unsupported default identity kind, or invalid default identity/local-profile combination. |
474	| `local_profile_not_found`    |       `404` | Requested local profile does not exist.                                                                                                           |
475	| `local_profiles_unavailable` |       `503` | Runtime is missing or the active store does not support local profile management.                                                                 |
477	## Internal Rails player-data HTTP calls
479	Authenticated Account stats are routed through the player-data Rails adapter when `PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_BASE_URL` is configured.
481	The adapter is an HTTP client inside `services/player-data`. The Rails API server owns the endpoints it calls.
483	### Authenticated-account stats read
485	```text
486	RailsStore.LoadStats
487	-> POST /api/internal/player-data/stats
488	```
490	Request body:
492	```json
493	{
494	  "account_id": "acct-..."
495	}
496	```
498	Required header:
500	```text
501	Authorization: Bearer <PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN>
502	```
504	Successful response:
506	```json
507	{
508	  "stats": {
509	    "total_score": 12,
510	    "high_score": 9,
511	    "ship_deaths": 3,
512	    "games_played": 4,
513	    "wins": 2
514	  }
515	}
516	```
518	The Rails adapter accepts only `identity_kind = authenticated_account` and requires a non-empty `account_id`.
520	`POST /api/internal/player-data/stats` is not the same as public `GET /api/player/stats`. The player-data route uses the internal stats endpoint by `account_id`.
522	### Authenticated-account match-result write
524	```text
525	RailsStore.RecordMatchResult
526	-> POST /internal/player-data/match-results
527	```
529	Request body:
531	```json
532	{
533	  "result_id": "result-1",
534	  "match_id": "match-1",
535	  "account_id": "acct-...",
536	  "score": 12,
537	  "ship_deaths": 2,
538	  "won": true
539	}
540	```
542	Required header:
544	```text
545	Authorization: Bearer <PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN>
546	```
548	Successful response:
550	```json
551	{
552	  "accepted": true,
553	  "duplicate": false,
554	  "stats": {
555	    "total_score": 12,
556	    "high_score": 12,
557	    "ship_deaths": 2,
558	    "games_played": 1,
559	    "wins": 1
560	  }
561	}
562	```
564	The Rails adapter requires:
566	```text
567	identity_kind = authenticated_account
568	account_id
569	result_id
570	match_id
571	PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN
572	```
574	A rejected Rails response with `accepted = false` is converted into a player-data store error.
576	## Token roles
578	User bearer tokens are used for authenticated-account profile reads through:
580	```text
581	POST /api/player-data/profile
582	```
584	The token proves the client’s authenticated account identity to the hosted player-data profile handler. The handler verifies the token through the game-server auth verifier adapter.
586	Internal bearer tokens are used for player-data service-to-Rails calls:
588	```text
589	POST /api/internal/player-data/stats
590	POST /internal/player-data/match-results
591	```
593	Those calls use:
595	```text
596	PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN
597	```
599	Static user bearer-token configuration such as `PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_BEARER_TOKEN` is not part of the current flow.
601	Local profile management routes do not require bearer-token auth in the current handler implementation.
603	## Lifecycle
605	### Game-server startup
607	```text
608	main()
609	  create HTTP mux
610	  create room manager
611	  build player-data runtime
612	  create player-data sink
613	  create match reporter
614	  build auth verifier
615	  mount health and WebSocket routes
616	  mount player-data HTTP routes
617	  listen on :8080
618	```
620	The game-server process fails startup if player-data runtime initialization fails.
622	The game-server process also fails startup if match reporter initialization fails.
624	### Runtime configuration
626	The game-server builds the player-data runtime from:
628	```text
629	PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_BASE_URL
630	PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN
631	playerDataLocalStorePath()
632	playerDataLocalStoreFactory()
633	```
635	Configured runtime routing:
637	| Identity kind           | Runtime route | Backing behavior                                                                                |
638	| ----------------------- | ------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
639	| `guest`                 | Guest store   | process-local guest memory                                                                      |
640	| `local_profile`         | Local store   | embedded SQLite in standard no-tag development builds                                           |
641	| `authenticated_account` | Account store | Rails adapter when Rails base URL is configured; in-memory account fallback when not configured |
643	In `noembeddedsqlite` builds, the embedded SQLite package and dependency are excluded. The local store path is unavailable, and local profile management returns `local_profiles_unavailable`.

992	## Notes
994	The current player-data HTTP facade is hosted in the game-server process, but its behavior remains a player-data service responsibility.
996	The hosted HTTP profile path and generated player-data packet path are related but not identical. The HTTP profile handler validates supported play-mode and identity-kind strings, resolves identity, and calls `Runtime.LoadStats` directly. The packet dispatcher validates mode/identity combinations before store access.
998	Local Profile display names are presentation values. `local_profile_id` is the local durable identity.
1000	Authenticated Account routing uses `account_id` resolved from token verification. Rails `user_id` remains an API-server internal database identifier.
1002	Guest stats are process-local and transient. Local Profile stats are durable only when local storage is configured. Authenticated Account stats route through the Rails adapter when Rails configuration is present.

===== docs/protocol/realtime-websocket-protocol.md =====
1	## Realtime WebSocket Protocol
3	Parent index: [Protocol](./!INDEX.md)
5	## Purpose
7	This document describes the current realtime WebSocket protocol between the Godot client and the Go game server.
9	The protocol is JSON-over-WebSocket for auth, room, lobby, signaling, and other pre-readiness/session-control packets, and JSON-over-lane-specific WebRTC DataChannels for active realtime gameplay and tooling packets: `sr.world`, `sr.overlay`, `sr.session`, `sr.event`, `sr.asteroids.lifecycle`, and `sr.bullets.lifecycle` are ordered/reliable, `sr.asteroids` and `sr.bullets` are unordered/unreliable hot-update lanes, and the mandatory `sr.tooling` lane is reliable/ordered/bidirectional with negotiated id 9.
11	It covers the transport route, JSON packet framing, connection lifecycle, packet-family routing, lane policy, gameplay packet families, session-state requirements, delivery semantics, source-of-truth files, generated outputs, service responsibilities, compatibility expectations, and implementation code paths.
13	## Overview
15	The realtime protocol currently uses JSON text messages over a WebSocket connection for signaling and queued one-off packets, and lane-specific WebRTC physical DataChannels for active realtime gameplay packets, with ordered/reliable lanes for `sr.world`, `sr.overlay`, `sr.session`, `sr.event`, `sr.asteroids.lifecycle`, and `sr.bullets.lifecycle`, unordered/unreliable hot-update lanes for `sr.asteroids` and `sr.bullets`, and a mandatory reliable/ordered/bidirectional `sr.tooling` transport lane at negotiated id 9.
17	The game server exposes one realtime route:
19	```text
20	GET /ws
21	```
23	The Godot client selects a WebSocket URL from the requested session mode, opens the connection, optionally sends an auth packet, sends room or gameplay request packets, and receives authoritative server packets. /ws is the signaling, session, and control route, not the active gameplay state transport.
25	WebSocket owns auth, room, lobby, signaling, and queued pre-readiness/session-control packets. WebRTC physical DataChannels own active realtime gameplay packets and runtime tooling packets. Current physical gameplay DataChannels are `sr.world`, `sr.overlay`, `sr.session`, `sr.event`, `sr.asteroids`, `sr.bullets`, `sr.asteroids.lifecycle`, and `sr.bullets.lifecycle`; `sr.tooling` is the ninth required channel and already carries runtime measurement. `control` is a logical recovery category, not a current physical WebRTC gameplay DataChannel. The current generated recovery packet families are `resync_request` and `resync_required`; there is no generated packet family named `control`, and there is no physical `sr.control` channel in the current implementation. The WebSocket URL may point at the normal hosted or proxied service route, but WebRTC DataChannel connectivity is established by ICE candidates rather than by a WebRTC URL. Deployment must allow the advertised WebRTC ICE address to reach the game server directly. Cloudflare-proxied HTTP routes should not be assumed to carry WebRTC DataChannel traffic.
27	The route path does not define play mode. Local single-player and multiplayer currently use the same local WebSocket route during development. Single-player versus multiplayer behavior is expressed through packets, session identity, room state, admission policy, and player-data routing.
29	The WebSocket connection itself is only transport readiness for WebSocket-owned packet families. It does not imply:
31	```text
32	authenticated account identity
33	room membership
34	ready state
35	active gameplay player state
36	durable Local Profile identity
37	durable account identity
38	```
40	The server owns authority behind accepted room, gameplay, auth-result, telemetry, and devtools consequences. The client owns connection initiation, packet emission, inbound packet classification, realtime packet application through RealtimePacketPipeline, presentation routing, and WebRTC transport consumption.
43	Active realtime gameplay packets are not WebSocket-owned anymore. The current server path builds the lane packet, encodes it to JSON, and sends it over the matching session WebRTC gameplay DataChannel when WebRTC is ready. WebSocket still owns auth, room/lobby lifecycle, room snapshots, and WebRTC signaling. There is no WebSocket fallback for active realtime gameplay packets.
44	The protocol is best-effort and session-scoped, but active gameplay output now uses lane-native packet families and lane policy over WebRTC DataChannels. The current gameplay output lanes are:
46	```text
47	world
48	asteroids
49	bullets
50	overlay
51	session
52	event
53	asteroids.lifecycle
54	bullets.lifecycle
55	```
57	`sr.tooling` is not an active gameplay packet lane. Its negotiated channel is mandatory for readiness, and tooling packets are separated before normal gameplay routing. Runtime measurement request/response traffic and runtime devtools command requests are implemented on this channel. Devtools command payloads carry `request_id` and `trace_id`; `services/game-server/internal/networking/tooling` applies packet-policy, room, and capability preflight, decodes `DebugCommand`, dispatches through the existing devtools controller, and returns correlated `tooling_command_result` or `tooling_error` packets. Telemetry routing exists but remains partially wired. Developer readouts now use `sr.tooling`; the legacy continuous telemetry/ping path remains to migrate.
59	`control` is a logical recovery category, not a current physical WebRTC gameplay DataChannel. The current generated recovery packet families are `resync_request` and `resync_required`. There is no separate generated packet family named `control`, and there is no physical `sr.control` channel in the current implementation.
61	The active gameplay packet families are:
63	```text
64	world_full
65	world_delta
66	asteroid_delta
67	bullet_delta
68	asteroids_lifecycle
69	bullets_lifecycle
70	overlay_full
71	overlay_delta
72	session_full
73	session_delta
74	event_batch
75	```
77	These packets carry current lane snapshots, baseline updates, and event batches instead of one combined lane gameplay output payload. `resync_request` and `resync_required` are WebSocket control packets, not active WebRTC lane outputs or `RealtimeLaneCandidate`s.
79	Every server-built realtime packet carries the authoritative `match_id` metadata (compact wire key `mid`). A `RealtimeSessionState` is bounded by the identity tuple `(ReceiverID, MatchID)`; changing either component starts clean lane, baseline, projection, and hot-lane cohort state.
81	WebSocket room snapshots and WebRTC gameplay packets have no cross-transport ordering guarantee. The client pipeline expands compact `mid` to readable `match_id` and validates recognized packet types before applying its match boundary. Before an authoritative active match exists, recognized packets with a non-empty `match_id` are buffered by match and do not mutate lane or presentation state; missing IDs are rejected. When the authoritative `InGame` room snapshot activates `begin_realtime_match(match_id)`, the pipeline clears all pending match buckets and replays only that match's packets through normal lane routing. Pending unrelated matches are discarded, and once a match is active, mismatched IDs are rejected. `GameOver` retains the active match until Lobby/session teardown; `end_match`, reset, and connection teardown clear pending packets and protocol state. This applies to all active packet families, including lifecycle and event packets.
83	## Canonical baseline recovery loop
85	`BaselineTracker` owns mismatch detection for the world, overlay, and session baselines. A delta with no usable baseline is rejected with `missing_baseline`; a delta with a different baseline is rejected with `wrong_baseline`. Ordinary stale or duplicate sequence rejection is silent. Only the first transition into pending recovery emits a request; repeated rejected deltas are deduplicated.
87	The client request contains `type`, `match_id`, `lane`, the last active `baseline_id` (or an empty string), the last accepted `sequence`, and `reason`. The queue envelope captures the room ID, receiver ID, and match ID alongside the request. Its path is `BaselineTracker -> RealtimeRouter -> RealtimePacketPipeline -> ClientConnectionService -> ClientPacketSender -> generated resync_request_packet -> NetworkClient -> WebSocket`. If WebSocket is closed, the request is not queued or automatically retried. The server write loop discards requests with missing, mismatched, or stale room, receiver, or match identities before writing or invalidating realtime state; a valid `resync_required` response carries the validated current `match_id`.
89	The server accepts only world, overlay, and session requests when the session has an active room, non-nil game instance, and active game player. The read path validates and queues a typed request without mutating `RealtimeSessionState`. Its queue envelope captures room ID, receiver/player ID, and match ID; the write loop discards requests whose context is stale, including requests from a previous match. It writes `resync_required` over WebSocket first, including the validated current match ID, then invalidates only the requested lane's readiness and projection after a successful write. Lane metadata and sequence are preserved and other lanes are untouched.
91	The next normal planner pass emits a full only for the invalidated lane at previous sequence plus one, with a new sequence-backed baseline ID. That full is a normal required world, overlay, or session candidate sent on the existing ordered/reliable WebRTC lane. Changed-baseline full chunks are accepted only while recovery is pending, with stable baseline ID, sequence, and chunk count, contiguous indices, and correct finality. Readiness remains false until the final chunk; the final accepted full clears tracker and `ResyncState` pending state and restores lane/gameplay readiness when all required lanes are synced.
93	WebSocket acknowledgment and WebRTC recovery full have no shared delivery order. `resync_required` is acknowledgment-only: it updates reason state only while the lane remains pending and is ignored after recovery, preventing delayed acknowledgment regression. Inbound `resync_request` remains a compatibility client route; the current server sends `resync_required`, not `resync_request`. Baseline recovery covers only world, overlay, and session baselines, not hot asteroid/bullet or lifecycle lanes, and does not add resend, retry, session resume, durable queues, or outbound client queueing.
95	## WebRTC transport recovery
97	The WebRTC transport foundation is implemented. An unexpected close of a previously-open required channel preserves the WebSocket connection, session, room membership, and game context. The client replaces only the WebRTC peer, negotiates a fresh offer, and uses a 10-second recovery deadline. Successful replacement readiness preserves the active match ID and requests fresh world, overlay, and session baselines through the existing recovery path. Recovery failure or timeout disables only single-player realtime replay; it does not reset multiplayer/session state. Runtime measurement already uses the recovered `sr.tooling` channel; subscription consumers must explicitly resubscribe after successful recovery.
99	## Participating systems

107	```text

122	```
124	Owns the server WebSocket upgrade, session object, read loop, write loop, inbound packet-family routing, outbound queue, room-session adapter wiring, auth session state, telemetry pong handling, room request routing, lane gameplay packet routing, WebRTC signaling, and disconnect cleanup.
126	```text

128	```
130	Owns the server JSON encode/decode wrapper used by networking.
